constraints: motion occurs on a horizontal plane
surface is rough
pulling force is constant
block starts from rest
kinetic friction acts during motion over distance s
relations: v_f = sqrt((2 * s * (F - mu * m * g)) / m)
assumptions: pulling force is applied parallel to the horizontal displacement
gravitational acceleration is constant with symbol g
problem_ir.goal_statement: Determine the final speed of the block after moving distance s on the rough horizontal plane using the work-kinetic-energy theorem.

coordinate_system.x_axis: horizontal along the direction of motion

reference_frame.description: ground frame attached to the horizontal plane

simplifications: no vertical displacement
weight and normal force do no work
only pulling force and kinetic friction contribute to net work

symbol_table.W_net: net work done on the block

known_quantities.description: moment of inertia of the disk about the fixed axis
initial angular velocity; starts from rest

unknown_target.description: angular velocity of the disk at time t = T

constraints: disk rotates about a fixed axis
applied torque is constant
initial condition: starts from rest
evaluate angular velocity at t = T
relations: for constant I and fixed axis: I*domega/dt = M
integrate from t=0 to t=T with omega(0)=0
assumptions: moment of inertia I remains constant
torque M acts about the rotation axis
problem_ir.goal_statement: Determine the angular velocity of the rigid disk at time t = T using the angular momentum theorem.

coordinate_system.positive_direction: same direction as applied torque M

reference_frame.description: frame in which the fixed rotation axis is at rest

simplifications: treat motion as one-dimensional rotation about a fixed axis
ignore any deformation of the disk

symbol_table.I: moment of inertia of the disk about the fixed axis
symbol_table.T: time interval during which torque is applied

known_quantities.description: known acceleration of the block along the incline upward

unknown_target.description: P: {'description': 'required pulling force along the incline'}

constraints: block moves on a smooth incline
no friction acts between block and incline
pulling force P acts along the incline
acceleration a is along the incline upward
relations: weight of block = m*g vertically downward
component of weight along incline = m*g*sin(alpha) downward along incline
normal reaction is perpendicular to incline
by D'Alembert's principle, inertial force m*a acts opposite the acceleration, i.e. down the incline
force balance along incline in dynamic equilibrium: P - m*g*sin(alpha) - m*a = 0
assumptions: block is treated as a particle
motion is constrained to the incline
D'Alembert's principle converts the accelerated motion to static equilibrium by adding inertial force opposite acceleration

described_elements: block on an incline of angle alpha
pulling force P directed up the incline

problem_ir.goal_statement: Determine the pulling force P required to pull the block up the smooth incline with acceleration a using D'Alembert's principle.

axes: x-axis along the incline, positive upward
y-axis perpendicular to the incline, positive outward from the plane

reference_frame.type: non-inertial formulation via D'Alembert's principle or equivalently inertial frame with Newton's second law
reference_frame.details: inertial force m*a is introduced opposite the actual acceleration

simplifications: friction neglected due to smooth incline
only force components along and perpendicular to incline are considered
rotational effects are neglected

symbol_table.a: acceleration of block up the incline

known_quantities.description: constant horizontal force acting on the particle
time duration for which the force acts

unknown_target.description: final velocity of the particle after time T

constraints: Particle moves along a straight line
Force is constant
Force acts horizontally
Force acts for a time interval T
All other forces are neglected
assumptions: The applied force is the net force
problem_ir.goal_statement: Use the momentum theorem to derive the final velocity of the particle after time T and the impulse delivered by the constant force.

coordinate_system.axis: x-axis along the straight-line motion and force direction

reference_frame.description: Laboratory frame in which the particle motion is observed

simplifications: Treat the particle as a point mass
Ignore gravity, friction, and any other external forces
Since force is constant and collinear with motion, use scalar 1D form

known_quantities.description: horizontal applied force for 0 <= t <= T

unknown_target.description: change in momentum during 0 <= t <= T

extra_targets.description: velocity of the cart at t = T

constraints: motion is along a horizontal frictionless track
force acts horizontally
relations: F(t) = F0*(1 - t/T) for 0 <= t <= T
assumptions: cart can be treated as a particle
motion is one-dimensional along the track
no friction force acts on the cart
vertical forces cancel and do not affect horizontal momentum
problem_ir.goal_statement: Use the momentum theorem to determine the cart's change in momentum over 0 <= t <= T and its velocity at t = T.

coordinate_system.positive_direction: along the initial motion / along the horizontal track

reference_frame.type: inertial frame fixed to the ground/track

simplifications: ignore rotational motion
consider only horizontal motion
treat the given force as the net horizontal force

symbol_table.T: final time / duration of force application
symbol_table.F0: initial magnitude parameter of applied force
symbol_table.delta_p: change in momentum from 0 to T

known_quantities.description: force acting along the x-axis as a function of position
speed of the particle at x = x1

unknown_target.description: speed of the particle at x = x2

constraints: Motion is along the x-axis only
Force depends on position: F(x) = a*x - b*x^3
Speed is known at x = x1
Find speed at x = x2 using the work-kinetic-energy theorem
relations: W_1to2 = integral from x1 to x2 of F(x) dx
integral from x1 to x2 of (a*x - b*x^3) dx = (a/2)*(x2^2 - x1^2) - (b/4)*(x2^4 - x1^4)
assumptions: The particle can be treated as a point mass
The given force is the net force along the x-axis
No other forces do work besides the specified force
problem_ir.goal_statement: Derive the speed of the particle at position x = x2 from the known speed v1 at x = x1 using the work-kinetic-energy theorem.

reference_frame.description: Laboratory frame aligned with the x-axis

simplifications: One-dimensional motion
Use speed magnitude rather than signed velocity
Force is expressed only as a function of position

symbol_table.F(x): force as a function of position
symbol_table.W_1to2: work done by the force from x1 to x2

problem_ir.goal_statement: Determine the angular velocity at t = T and the total angular displacement from t = 0 to t = T using the angular momentum theorem.

known_quantities.description: time interval during which the tangential force acts

relations.description: initial angular momentum magnitude about O for tangential motion on a circle
moment about O due to tangential force
change in angular momentum equals angular impulse about O
angular momentum-speed relation for circular tangential motion
tangential speed at time T obtained from angular momentum relation

assumptions: The tangential force acts in the direction of motion so its moment increases the angular momentum
The particle remains constrained to the circular path of radius r during the interval
Angular momentum is evaluated about the center O

simplifications: Only the tangential force contributes to the moment about O

Radius r is constant

rod is massless
motion is in a horizontal plane
rotation is about fixed point O at one end of the rod
impulse is tangential

impulse is applied instantaneously to the mass at the free end

relations.description: angular impulse about O from tangential impulse at distance l
angular momentum of rigid system about fixed point O
moment of inertia of point masses about O

assumptions: system behaves as a rigid body during and immediately after the impulse

weight and other non-impulsive forces have negligible angular impulse during the short impact interval
initial angular velocity is not specified
problem_ir.goal_statement: Use the theorem of moment of momentum about O to find the change in angular momentum of the two-mass rod system and the angular velocity immediately after the tangential impulse is applied.

objects.name: incline

objects.name: string
objects.description: light string connecting m1 and m2

known_quantities.value: kinetic friction coefficient between m1 and incline
instantaneous acceleration magnitude of the system

constraints: m1 is moving up the incline at the instant considered
kinetic friction acts on m1 opposite its motion, i.e. down the incline
string is light
pulley is frictionless
connected masses have the same acceleration magnitude
relations: for m1 along incline: T - m1*g*sin(alpha) - f_k - m1*a = 0 (D'Alembert form, taking up the incline as positive)
for m1 normal to incline: N - m1*g*cos(alpha) = 0
assumptions: acceleration of m1 is up the incline
kinetic friction coefficient mu is constant
no mass or stretch in the string
pulley only changes direction of the string tension

m1_forces: normal reaction N perpendicular to incline
kinetic friction f_k = mu*N down the incline
diagram_information.motion: m1 moves up the incline, so m2 moves downward

problem_ir.goal_statement: Determine the string tension T and the normal reaction N on m1 using D'Alembert's principle.

for_m1.y_axis: perpendicular to the incline, positive outward from the plane

problem_ir.reference_frame: non-rotating frame attached to the ground; D'Alembert's principle applied by adding inertia force opposite the acceleration
simplifications: treat bodies as particles for translational force balance
ignore pulley inertia
ignore air resistance

exact trigonometric dependence retained

simplifications: frictionless cart motion
ideal spring
point-mass pendulum bob
fixed pendulum length

known_quantities.description: displacement measured from static equilibrium position

constraints: motion is along a horizontal guide
guide is frictionless
x is measured from the static equilibrium position
free vibration
relations: x(t) = C1 cos(omega_n t) + C2 sin(omega_n t)
problem_ir.goal_statement: Derive the governing free-vibration differential equation for the mass-spring system, determine the natural circular frequency, and write the general solution for displacement as a function of time.
simplifications: because displacement is measured from static equilibrium, constant equilibrium forces are eliminated from the equation
frictionless guide implies no resistive force

objects.name: mass-spring-damper oscillator
objects.excitation: external harmonic force F0 cos(Omega t)

unknown_target.description: primary: ['equation of motion', 'steady-state amplitude X(Omega)', 'condition for resonance-like peak response']

constraints: single-degree-of-freedom linear damped forced vibration
external force is harmonic: F(t) = F0 cos(Omega t)
steady-state response requested as a function of Omega
relations: Assume steady-state response x_ss(t) = X*cos(Omega*t - phi)
Resonance-like peak occurs when damping is sufficiently small, specifically zeta < 1/sqrt(2)
assumptions: transient response is neglected for amplitude expression
problem_ir.goal_statement: Write the equation of motion for the damped forced oscillator, derive the steady-state amplitude as a function of forcing frequency Omega, and state the condition for occurrence of a resonance-like peak response.
simplifications: single-degree-of-freedom model
ignore nonlinearities
ignore any base motion
use steady-state solution only for amplitude-frequency relation

    "m*x_ddot + c*x_dot + k*x = F0*cos(Omega*t)",
    "Assume steady-state response x_ss(t) = X*cos(Omega*t - phi)",

    "phi = atan2(c*Omega, k - m*Omega^2)",

    "zeta = c / (2*sqrt(k*m))",
    "X(Omega) = (F0/k) / sqrt((1 - (Omega/omega_n)^2)^2 + (2*zeta*Omega/omega_n)^2)",
    "Resonance-like peak occurs when damping is sufficiently small, specifically zeta < 1/sqrt(2)"
